---
title: "Table Compatibility Test: Word & Google Docs"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: true
    self-contained: true
  docx: default
editor: visual
---

# Overview

This notebook tests R table packages for compatibility with Word and Google
Docs. The goal is to find the best replacement for `gt` tables that preserves
formatting when exported to Word (.docx) or copied to Google Docs.

## Test Requirements

1.  **Formatting preservation**: Fonts, colors, borders, alignment
2.  **Number formatting**: Integers, decimals, percentages
3.  **Grouped tables**: Multiple grouping levels
4.  **Styling flexibility**: Custom themes similar to `theme_gt_manuscript()`
5.  **Markdown/Quarto integration**: Works in both Rmarkdown and Quarto

## Packages Tested

-   **flextable**: Best Office integration, direct Word support
-   **kableExtra**: Popular, good HTML/PDF, limited Word support
-   **huxtable**: Cross-format tables with consistent API
-   **officer + flextable**: Direct Word document manipulation

```{r setup, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
library(tidyverse)
library(gt)  # For comparison

# Install test packages if needed (run once)
# install.packages(c("flextable", "kableExtra", "huxtable", "officer"))

library(flextable)
library(kableExtra)
library(huxtable)
library(officer)
```

```{r sample-data}
# Create sample data mimicking project tables
sample_data <- tibble::tribble(
  ~benchmark, ~ref, ~bench_type, ~total_bp, ~variant_count, ~density,
  "v4.2.1", "GRCh38", "smvar", 2857433814, 3486393, 1.22,
  "v5.0q", "GRCh38", "smvar", 2863320957, 3512847, 1.23,
  "v5.0q", "CHM13v2.0", "smvar", 2910837291, 3548921, 1.22,
  "v4.2.1", "GRCh38", "stvar", 2857433814, 11234, 0.0039,
  "v5.0q", "GRCh38", "stvar", 2863320957, 12456, 0.0044,
  "v5.0q", "CHM13v2.0", "stvar", 2910837291, 13102, 0.0045
)

# Create grouped data
grouped_data <- sample_data %>%
  group_by(ref) %>%
  slice_head(n = 3) %>%
  ungroup()

print(sample_data)
```

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 1. flextable (RECOMMENDED)

**Pros:**

-   Excellent Word/Office integration via `officer` package
-   Native .docx export with preserved formatting
-   Flexible styling API similar to `gt`
-   Good HTML output for web viewing
-   Active development and maintenance

**Cons:**

-   Different API from `gt` (requires code refactoring)
-   Slightly verbose syntax compared to `gt`

## Basic flextable

```{r flextable-basic}
ft_basic <- sample_data %>%
  flextable() %>%
  add_header_lines("Basic flextable Example")

ft_basic
```

## Styled flextable (Manuscript Theme)

```{r flextable-styled}
# Custom theme mimicking theme_gt_manuscript
theme_flextable_manuscript <- function(ft) {
  ft %>%
    # Font styling
    font(fontname = "Roboto", part = "all") %>%
    fontsize(size = 9, part = "body") %>%
    fontsize(size = 10, part = "header") %>%
    
    # Borders
    border_remove() %>%
    hline_top(border = fp_border(width = 2, color = "black"), part = "all") %>%
    hline_bottom(border = fp_border(width = 2, color = "black"), part = "all") %>%
    hline(i = 1, border = fp_border(width = 1, color = "black"), part = "header") %>%
    
    # Alignment
    align(align = "center", part = "header") %>%
    align(j = c("Benchmark", "Reference", "Type"), align = "left", part = "body") %>%
    align(j = c("Total Bases", "Variants", "Density (var/Mb)"), align = "right", part = "body") %>%
    
    # Background colors
    bg(bg = "#F5F5F5", part = "header") %>%
    
    # Striped rows (alternate rows)
    bg(i = which(seq_len(nrow(ft$body$content)) %% 2 == 1), bg = "#FAFAFA", part = "body") %>%
    
    # Padding
    padding(padding = 8, part = "all") %>%
    
    # Column width
    autofit()
}

ft_styled <- sample_data %>%
  rename(
    Benchmark = benchmark,
    Reference = ref,
    Type = bench_type,
    `Total Bases` = total_bp,
    Variants = variant_count,
    `Density (var/Mb)` = density
  ) %>%
  flextable() %>%
  add_header_lines("Styled flextable with Manuscript Theme") %>%
  
  # Format numbers
  colformat_int(j = c("Total Bases", "Variants"), big.mark = ",") %>%
  colformat_double(j = "Density (var/Mb)", digits = 4) %>%
  
  # Apply custom theme
  theme_flextable_manuscript()

ft_styled
```

## Grouped flextable

```{r flextable-grouped}
ft_grouped <- grouped_data %>%
  rename(
    Benchmark = benchmark,
    Reference = ref,
    Type = bench_type,
    `Total Bases` = total_bp,
    Variants = variant_count,
    `Density (var/Mb)` = density
  ) %>%
  arrange(Reference, Type) %>%
  flextable() %>%
  
  # Format numbers
  colformat_int(j = c("Total Bases", "Variants"), big.mark = ",") %>%
  colformat_double(j = "Density (var/Mb)", digits = 4) %>%
  
  # Group by reference
  theme_flextable_manuscript() %>%
  merge_v(j = "Reference") %>%
  fix_border_issues()

ft_grouped
```

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 2. kableExtra

**Pros:**

-   Simple API, works with base `kable()`
-   Good HTML output with Bootstrap styling
-   Many styling options

**Cons:**

-   **Limited Word support**: Many styles lost in .docx export
-   Primarily designed for HTML and LaTeX/PDF
-   Not ideal for Word/Google Docs workflow

## Basic kableExtra

```{r kableExtra-basic}
sample_data %>%
  kable(
    format = "html",
    caption = "Basic kableExtra Example",
    col.names = c("Benchmark", "Reference", "Type", "Total Bases", "Variants", "Density")
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"),
    full_width = FALSE
  )
```

## Styled kableExtra

```{r kableExtra-styled}
sample_data %>%
  kable(
    format = "html",
    caption = "Styled kableExtra (Note: Limited Word Export)",
    col.names = c("Benchmark", "Reference", "Type", "Total Bases", "Variants", "Density"),
    digits = c(0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 4)
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "condensed"),
    full_width = FALSE,
    font_size = 12
  ) %>%
  row_spec(0, bold = TRUE, background = "#F5F5F5") %>%
  column_spec(1:3, width = "8em") %>%
  column_spec(4:6, width = "10em")
```

**Note:** When this renders to Word, most Bootstrap styling is lost. kableExtra
is best for HTML/PDF outputs, not Word/Google Docs.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 3. huxtable

**Pros:**

-   Unified API for HTML, LaTeX, Word, and screen output
-   Good Word compatibility via `officer`
-   Consistent appearance across output formats

**Cons:**

-   More verbose than `gt` or `flextable`
-   Smaller user community
-   Occasional formatting quirks

## Basic huxtable

```{r huxtable-basic}
hux_basic <- sample_data %>%
  as_hux(add_colnames = TRUE) %>%
  set_caption("Basic huxtable Example")

hux_basic
```

## Styled huxtable

```{r huxtable-styled}
hux_styled <- sample_data %>%
  as_hux(add_colnames = TRUE) %>%
  set_caption("Styled huxtable with Manuscript Theme") %>%
  
  # Header styling
  set_bold(1, everywhere, TRUE) %>%
  set_background_color(1, everywhere, "#F5F5F5") %>%
  set_all_borders(1, everywhere, 1) %>%
  set_top_border(1, everywhere, 2) %>%
  set_bottom_border(1, everywhere, 1) %>%
  
  # Body styling
  set_all_padding(everywhere, everywhere, 4) %>%
  set_font_size(everywhere, everywhere, 9) %>%
  set_valign(everywhere, everywhere, "middle") %>%
  
  # Striped rows
  set_background_color(evens, everywhere, "#FAFAFA") %>%
  
  # Bottom border
  set_bottom_border(final(1), everywhere, 2) %>%
  
  # Column alignment
  set_align(everywhere, 4:6, "right") %>%
  
  # Number formatting
  set_number_format(everywhere, 4:5, 0) %>%
  set_number_format(everywhere, 6, 4) %>%
  
  # Column width
  set_width(0.9)

hux_styled
```

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 4. gt Table (Current Approach)

For comparison, here's how tables currently look with `gt`:

```{r gt-example}
# Source the theme function
source(here::here("R/plot_themes.R"))

gt_table <- sample_data %>%
  gt() %>%
  fmt_integer(columns = c(total_bp, variant_count)) %>%
  fmt_number(columns = density, decimals = 4) %>%
  cols_label(
    benchmark = "Benchmark",
    ref = "Reference",
    bench_type = "Type",
    total_bp = "Total Bases",
    variant_count = "Variants",
    density = "Density (var/Mb)"
  ) %>%
  theme_gt_manuscript()

gt_table
```

**Note:** `gt` tables look excellent in HTML but lose most formatting when
exported to Word or copied to Google Docs. This is the problem we're solving.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# 5. Direct Word Document Creation with officer

For maximum control, you can create Word documents directly using `officer` +
`flextable`:

```{r word-doc-creation, eval=FALSE}
# Create a Word document with embedded flextable
doc <- read_docx() %>%
  body_add_par("Sample Table Report", style = "heading 1") %>%
  body_add_par("This demonstrates direct Word document creation.", style = "Normal") %>%
  body_add_flextable(ft_styled) %>%
  body_add_par("", style = "Normal") %>%
  body_add_par("Additional tables can be added sequentially.", style = "Normal") %>%
  body_add_flextable(ft_grouped)

# Save to file
print(doc, target = "table_test_output.docx")

message("Word document created: table_test_output.docx")
```

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Recommendations

## Best Overall: flextable + officer

**Recommended workflow:**

1.  **Create tables with `flextable`** using a custom
    `theme_flextable_manuscript()` function
2.  **Render Quarto/Rmarkdown to Word** using `format: docx`
3.  **For Google Docs**: Open .docx in Google Docs (formatting mostly preserved)
    OR copy tables from Word
4.  **For custom reports**: Use `officer` to build Word documents
    programmatically

### Migration Strategy from gt

Create a wrapper function to ease migration:

```{r migration-helper}
#' Convert gt-style workflow to flextable
#' 
#' @param data Data frame
#' @param ... Additional flextable customizations
convert_gt_to_flextable <- function(data, ...) {
  data %>%
    flextable() %>%
    theme_flextable_manuscript() %>%
    ...
}

# Example usage (mimics gt syntax)
sample_data %>%
  convert_gt_to_flextable() %>%
  colformat_int(j = c("total_bp", "variant_count")) %>%
  colformat_double(j = "density", digits = 4)
```

## Comparison Matrix

| Feature | flextable | kableExtra | huxtable | gt |
|----------------|----------------|-----------------|----------------|----------------|
| Word Export | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Excellent | ⭐⭐ Poor | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ Good | ⭐ Very Poor |
| Google Docs | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ Good | ⭐⭐ Poor | ⭐⭐⭐ Fair | ⭐ Very Poor |
| HTML Output | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ Good | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Excellent | ⭐⭐⭐ Fair | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Excellent |
| Styling Control | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Excellent | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ Good | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ Good | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Excellent |
| API Simplicity | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ Good | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Simple | ⭐⭐⭐ Moderate | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Excellent |
| Documentation | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Excellent | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ Good | ⭐⭐⭐ Fair | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Excellent |

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Testing Instructions

1.  **Render this notebook:**

    ``` r
    quarto::quarto_render("tests/table_compatibility_test.qmd", output_format = "all")
    ```

2.  **Test Word export:**

    -   Open the generated `.docx` file
    -   Check formatting preservation (fonts, colors, borders, alignment)
    -   Try editing and confirming no corruption

3.  **Test Google Docs:**

    -   Method 1: Upload `.docx` to Google Drive → Open with Google Docs
    -   Method 2: Copy table from Word → Paste into Google Doc
    -   Method 3: Copy table from HTML output → Paste into Google Doc

4.  **Compare outputs:**

    -   Which tables maintain formatting best?
    -   Which workflow is most practical for your team?

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Next Steps

If flextable is chosen:

1.  Create `R/flextable_themes.R` with manuscript theme functions
2.  Update existing analysis notebooks to use `flextable` instead of `gt`
3.  Add flextable + officer to renv dependencies
4.  Document the new table workflow in project guidelines
5.  Consider keeping `gt` for HTML-only outputs (website, supplementary
    materials)
